// Module
var code = "<h1 id=calculations>Calculations</h1> <p>This page demonstrates calculations to determine the design strength of the WT tension member, considering the limit states of:</p> <ul> <li>Tensile yielding</li> <li>Tensile rupture</li> <li>Block shear rupture</li> </ul> <p>The calculations are based on the 2016 AISC <em>Specification</em> and the 15th edition AISC <em>Manual</em>. <span class=\"math inline\">\\(\\newcommand{\\inch}{~{\\rm{in.}}} \\newcommand{\\kips}{~{\\rm{kips}}} \\newcommand{\\ksi}{~{\\rm{ksi}}} \\newcommand{\\sixt}{{\\tfrac{1}{16}\\inch}}\\)</span></p> <h2 id=material-and-geometric-properties>Material and Geometric Properties</h2> <p>The connection consists of a WT8x25 with the flange connected by six (6) 1\" diameter A490X bolts to a 1\" plate.</p> <figure> <img src=./bolted-wt-top.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Structural drawing</figcaption> </figure> <p>Typically, the material specifications for each type of steel shape used on a project are listed on the general notes sheet of the structural drawings. For this case, the WT conforms to ASTM A992.</p> <p>From AISC <em>Manual</em> Table 2-4, the material properties for the WT are as follows:</p> <div class=properties> <table> <tbody> <tr class=odd> <td>ASTM A992</td> </tr> <tr class=even> <td><span class=\"math inline\">\\(F_y = 50\\ksi\\)</span></td> </tr> <tr class=odd> <td><span class=\"math inline\">\\(F_u = 65\\ksi\\)</span></td> </tr> </tbody> </table> </div> <p>From AISC <em>Manual</em> Table 1-8, the geometric properties are as follows:</p> <div class=properties> <table> <tbody> <tr class=odd> <td>WT8x25</td> <td></td> <td></td> </tr> <tr class=even> <td><span class=\"math inline\">\\(A_g = 7.37~\\rm{in.}^2\\)</span></td> <td><span class=\"math inline\">\\(t_f = 0.630~\\rm{in.}\\)</span></td> <td><span class=\"math inline\">\\(d = 8.13~\\rm{in.}\\)</span></td> </tr> <tr class=odd> <td><span class=\"math inline\">\\(\\bar{y} = 1.89~\\rm{in.}\\)</span></td> <td><span class=\"math inline\">\\(b_f = 7.07~\\rm{in.}\\)</span></td> <td><span class=\"math inline\">\\(t_w = 0.380~\\rm{in.}\\)</span></td> </tr> </tbody> </table> </div> <h2 id=tensile-yielding>Tensile Yielding</h2> <p>Calculate the nominal strength using AISC <em>Specification</em> Equation D2-1.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} P_n &amp;= F_y A_g \\\\ &amp;= (50 \\text{ ksi})(7.37 \\text{ in.}^2) \\\\ &amp;= 368 \\text{ kips} \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the design strength by applying the resistance factor.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\phi P_n = 0.9 (368 \\text { kips}) \\boxed{= 332 \\text{ kips}} \\]</span></p> <h2 id=tensile-rupture>Tensile Rupture</h2> <p>Steps:</p> <ul> <li>Calculate net area <span class=\"math inline\">\\(A_n\\)</span></li> <li>Calculate shear lag factor <span class=\"math inline\">\\(U\\)</span> and effective net area <span class=\"math inline\">\\(A_e\\)</span></li> <li>Calculate tensile rupture strength</li> </ul> <p>A depiction of tensile rupture failure for this connection is shown in <a href=#Tensile-rupture>this three-dimensional view</a>.</p> <p>The net area, <span class=\"math inline\">\\(A_n\\)</span>, is shown in red. It is equal to the gross area minus the material removed for two bolt holes as defined in Section B4.3b of the AISC <em>Specification</em>.</p> <figure> <img src=./wt-rupture-area.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Area in tension. Light areas are removed for net area.</figcaption> </figure> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_n &amp;= A_g - 2(d_h + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) t_f \\\\ &amp;= 7.37 \\text{ in.}^2 - 2 (1 \\tfrac{1}{8} \\text{ in.} + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) (0.630 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 5.87 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>From the description of the element in AISC <em>Specification</em> Table D3.1 Case 2, calculate the shear lag factor <span class=\"math inline\">\\(U\\)</span> as the larger of the values from Case 2 and Case 7.</p> <p>Case 2, where:</p> <ul> <li><span class=\"math inline\">\\(\\bar{x}\\)</span> is <span class=\"math inline\">\\(\\bar{y}\\)</span> from AISC <em>Manual</em> Table 1-9.</li> <li><span class=\"math inline\">\\(l\\)</span> is the length of the connection, in the direction of the force, from the centerline of the first row of bolts to the centerline of the last row of bolts</li> </ul> <figure> <img src=./wt-shear-lag.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Figure showing <span class=\"math inline\">\\(l\\)</span></figcaption> </figure> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} U &amp;= 1 - \\frac{\\bar{x}}{l} \\\\ &amp;= 1 - \\frac{1.89 \\text{ in.}}{6 \\text{ in.}} \\\\ &amp;= 0.685 \\end{align}\\]</span></p> <p>Case 7; flange has three fasteners per line in the direction of loading:</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} b_f &amp;= 7.07\\text{ in.} \\\\ \\tfrac23 d &amp;= \\tfrac23 (8.13\\text{ in.}) = 5.42\\text{ in.} \\\\ b_f &amp;\\ge \\tfrac23 d \\Rightarrow \\boxed{U = 0.90} \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Use the larger <span class=\"math inline\">\\(U = 0.90\\)</span>.</p> <p>Calculate the effective net area using AISC <em>Specification</em> Equation D3-1:</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ A_e = A_n U = (5.87 \\text{ in.}^2) (0.90) = 5.29 \\text{ in.}^2 \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the nominal strength using AISC <em>Specification</em> Equation D2-2:</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} P_n &amp;= F_u A_e \\\\ &amp;= (65 \\text{ ksi})(5.29 \\text{ in.}^2) \\\\ &amp;= 344 \\text{ kips} \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the design strength by applying the resistance factor.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[\\phi P_n = 0.75 (344 \\text{ kips}) \\boxed{= 258 \\text{ kips}}\\]</span></p> <h2 id=block-shear>Block Shear</h2> <p>There are three plausible block shear paths for this connection:</p> <ol type=1> <li><a href=#Block-shear-1>Tension in the flange and shear in the stem</a></li> <li><a href=#Block-shear-2>Tension in the outside of the flange and shear along the bolt lines</a></li> <li><a href=#Block-shear-3>Tension in the stem and the inside of the flange and shear along the bolt lines</a></li> </ol> <p>All plausible paths have uniform tension stress, so <span class=\"math inline\">\\(U_{bs} = 1.0\\)</span> for all.</p> <h3 id=block-shear-1-model>Block Shear 1 (<a href=#Block-shear-1>model</a>)</h3> <figure> <img src=./wt-block-shear-1-area-shear.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Area subject to shear in blue.</figcaption> </figure> <p>The shear plane is in the stem at the toe of the fillet. Since there is no material removed (e.g., bolt holes), the gross and net shear areas <span class=\"math inline\">\\(A_{gv}\\)</span> and <span class=\"math inline\">\\(A_{nv}\\)</span> are the same:</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{gv} = A_{nv} &amp;= t_w (8 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= (0.380 \\text{ in.})(8 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 3.04 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>The gross area subject to tension is most easily calculated by subtracting the area of the stem from the gross area of the WT:</p> <figure> <img src=./wt-block-shear-1-area-tension.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Area subject to tension in red. Light areas are removed for net area.</figcaption> </figure> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{gt} &amp;= A - t_w (d - k_{des}) \\\\ &amp;= 7.37 \\text{ in.}^2 - (0.380 \\text{ in.})( 8.13 \\text{ in.} - 1.03 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 4.67 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the net area subject to tension.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{nt} &amp;= A_{gt} - 2(d_h + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) t_f \\\\ &amp;= 4.67 \\text{ in.}^2 - 2 (1 \\tfrac{1}{8} \\text{ in.} + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) (0.630 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 3.18 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the nominal strength using AISC <em>Specification</em> Equation J4-5. Since <span class=\"math inline\">\\(A_{gv} = A_{nv}\\)</span>, the strength with shear yield will control over the strength with shear rupture.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} R_n &amp;= 0.60 F_y A_{gv} + U_{bs} F_u A_{nt} \\\\ &amp;= 0.60 (50\\text{ ksi})(3.04\\text{ in.}^2) + 1.0 (65\\text{ ksi}) (3.18 \\text{ in.}^2)\\\\ &amp;\\boxed{= 298 \\text{ kips}} \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <h3 id=block-shear-2-model>Block Shear 2 (<a href=#Block-shear-2>model</a>)</h3> <figure> <img src=./wt-block-shear-2-path.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Block shear path 2.</figcaption> </figure> <p>Calculate the gross area subject to shear.</p> <figure> <img src=./wt-block-shear-2-area-shear.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Area subject to shear in blue. Light areas are removed for net area.</figcaption> </figure> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{gv} &amp;= 2 t_f (8 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 2 (0.630 \\text{ in.}) (8 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 10.1 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the net area by removing the material from 4 whole bolt holes and 2 half bolt holes.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{nv} &amp;= A_{gv} - 5 (d_h + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) t_f \\\\ &amp;= 10.1 \\text{ in.}^2 - 5 (1 \\tfrac18 \\text{ in.} + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) (0.630 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 6.34 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the gross area subject to tension.</p> <figure> <img src=./wt-block-shear-2-area-tension.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Area subject to tension in red. Light areas are removed for net area.</figcaption> </figure> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{gt} &amp;= (b_f - 3.5\\text{ in.}) t_f \\\\ &amp;= (7.07\\text{ in.} - 3.5\\text{ in.}) (0.630 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 2.25 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the net area subject to tension.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{nt} &amp;= A_{gt} - (d_h + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) t_f \\\\ &amp;= 2.25 \\text{ in.}^2 - (1 \\tfrac18 \\text{ in.} + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) (0.630 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 1.50 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the nominal block shear strength.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} R_n &amp;= 0.60 F_u A_{nv} + U_{bs} F_u A_{nt} \\le 0.60 F_y A_{gv} + U_{bs} F_u A_{nt} \\\\ &amp;= 0.60 (65\\text{ ksi}) (6.34 \\text{ in.}^2) + 1.0 (65\\text{ ksi}) (1.50 \\text{ in.}^2) \\\\ &amp;\\quad\\le 0.60 (50\\text{ ksi}) (10.1 \\text{ in.}^2) + 1.0 (65\\text{ ksi}) (1.50 \\text{ in.}^2) \\\\ &amp;= 247\\text{ kips} + 97.5\\text{ kips} \\le 303\\text{ kips} + 97.5\\text{ kips} \\\\ &amp;\\boxed{= 345\\text{ kips}} \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <h3 id=block-shear-3-model>Block Shear 3 (<a href=#Block-shear-3>model</a>)</h3> <figure> <img src=./wt-block-shear-3-path.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Block shear path 3.</figcaption> </figure> <p>The gross and net areas subject to shear are the same as for block shear 2.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{gv} &amp;= 10.1 \\text{ in.}^2\\\\ A_{nv} &amp;= 6.34 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the gross area subject to tension.</p> <figure> <img src=./wt-block-shear-3-area-tension.svg alt=\"\"/><figcaption>Area subject to tension in red. Light areas are removed for net area.</figcaption> </figure> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{gt} &amp;= A - (b_f - 3.5\\text{ in.}) t_f \\\\ &amp;= 7.37 \\text{ in.}^2 - (7.07\\text{ in.} - 3.5\\text{ in.}) (0.630 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 5.12 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the net area subject to tension.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} A_{nt} &amp;= A_{gt} - (d_h + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) t_f \\\\ &amp;= 5.12 \\text{ in.}^2 - (1 \\tfrac18 \\text{ in.} + \\tfrac{1}{16}\\text{ in.}) (0.630 \\text{ in.}) \\\\ &amp;= 4.37 \\text{ in.}^2 \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <p>Calculate the nominal block shear strength.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} R_n &amp;= 0.60 F_u A_{nv} + U_{bs} F_u A_{nt} \\le 0.60 F_y A_{gv} + U_{bs} F_u A_{nt} \\\\ &amp;= 0.60 (65\\text{ ksi}) (6.34 \\text{ in.}^2) + 1.0 (65\\text{ ksi}) (5.12 \\text{ in.}^2) \\\\ &amp;\\quad\\le 0.60 (50\\text{ ksi}) (10.1 \\text{ in.}^2) + 1.0 (65\\text{ ksi}) (5.12 \\text{ in.}^2) \\\\ &amp;= 247\\text{ kips} + 333\\text{ kips} \\le 303\\text{ kips} + 333\\text{ kips} \\\\ &amp;\\boxed{= 531\\text{ kips}} \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <h3 id=block-shear-summary>Block Shear Summary</h3> <p>The controlling nominal strength is from block shear 1, with <span class=\"math inline\">\\(R_n = 298\\text{ kips}\\)</span>. Calculate the design strength by applying the resistance factor.</p> <p><span class=\"math display\">\\[ \\begin{align} \\phi R_n &amp;= 0.75 (298\\text{ kips}) \\\\ &amp;\\boxed{= 223\\text{ kips}} \\end{align} \\]</span></p> <h2 id=limit-state-summary>Limit State Summary</h2> <p>The design tensile strength of the WT member is summarized in the following table.</p> <table> <caption>Tensile limit state summary</caption> <thead> <tr class=header> <th style=text-align:left>Limit state</th> <th style=text-align:right>Design strength</th> </tr> </thead> <tbody> <tr class=odd> <td style=text-align:left>Tensile yielding</td> <td style=text-align:right>332 kips</td> </tr> <tr class=even> <td style=text-align:left>Tensile rupture</td> <td style=text-align:right>258 kips</td> </tr> <tr class=odd> <td style=text-align:left>Block shear rupture</td> <td style=text-align:right>223 kips</td> </tr> </tbody> </table> <p><strong>Block shear rupture controls with a design strength of 223 kips.</strong></p> ";
// Exports
module.exports = code;
